title: For the Glory of God and the Good of Each Other
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: For The Glory Of God And The Good Of Each Other - Pastor Tito Lyro - 1 Peter 4 7 - 11.txt

outline.title: The Basis for Godly Living: The Return of Christ
subpoints: Verse 7: 'The end of all things is at hand' - Christ's return is the doctrinal foundation for all the commands that follow
All major events in God's plan of redemption have occurred; only the gathering of the fullness of the Gentiles and the elect remains
Throughout the New Testament, the return of Christ is given as a motive for godly living today, not a reason to escape the present
John Calvin: 'Christianity is a doctrine, not of the tongue, but of life' - doctrine is meant to be lived, not merely known

outline.title: The Goal of Godly Living: The Glory of God
subpoints: Verse 11: 'that in all things God may be glorified through Jesus Christ' - the purpose of life is doxology
The Westminster Shorter Catechism: man's chief end is to glorify God and to enjoy Him forever
1 Corinthians 10:31: whether you eat or drink or whatever you do, do all to the glory of God
If an activity cannot be done for the glory of God, it should not be done at all

outline.title: Godly Living in Prayer
subpoints: Verse 7: 'be serious and watchful in your prayers' - Peter assumes Christians are already praying and gives instruction on how to pray
Both words (serious and watchful) relate to a clear, uncluttered mind in prayer - the opposite of chanting, repetition, or anxious distraction
Prayer is to the Christian as breathing is to the human - something we simply do
Since Christ is returning, pray seriously, with an engaged and focused mind

outline.title: Godly Living in Love and Hospitality
subpoints: Verse 8: 'above all things have fervent love for one another, for love will cover a multitude of sins'
Love is capable of being commanded because it is not primarily an emotion but a decision of the will leading to action
The love called for is 'fervent' - ektenous - meaning fire-consuming, burning; it forgives and thinks the best of others
Verse 9: hospitality without grumbling - giving ourselves to others, being inconvenienced for the sake of others, is a major component of Christian life

outline.title: Godly Living Through Spiritual Gifts
subpoints: Verses 10-11: every believer has received a gift - a talent, skill, or capability to be used for the good of others
God lavishly supplies the strength and grace for all service; He does not demand what He does not first provide
Speaking gifts (especially teaching) must be exercised as 'the oracles of God' - only speaking what Scripture speaks, not one's own words
Serving gifts (deacon-type service) include caring for the physical and practical needs of the church - helping the poor, the widow, the sick

summary:
In this morning sermon on 1 Peter 4:7-11, Pastor Tito Lyro presents Peter's vision for the life of every Christian: to live for the glory of God and the good of each other. The sermon unfolds in two directions - from the foundational truth that grounds the call to godly living, and toward the specific practices that express it.

Pastor Lyro begins with the doctrinal basis: 'the end of all things is at hand.' Throughout the New Testament, Christ's return is not presented as an escape from present responsibilities but as the very reason to live faithfully and lovingly now. All the major events of redemption have occurred; the church awaits only the completion of the elect and the return of its Lord. John Calvin's observation anchors this: Christianity is a doctrine of life, not of the tongue, and every doctrine - including eschatology - is given to be lived, not merely debated.

The goal of this godly life is stated plainly in verse 11: 'that in all things God may be glorified through Jesus Christ.' Our lives are to be doxologies - ascriptions of glory to God in everything we do, from the mundane (eating and drinking, as 1 Corinthians 10:31 says) to the most significant relationships and responsibilities.

Peter then organizes this godly living around four other-oriented practices. Prayer: pray seriously and watchfully, with a clear, focused mind uncluttered by worldly anxiety or religious vain repetition. Love: exercise a consuming, burning love for fellow believers that covers and forgives offenses - a love that is a command of the will, not merely a feeling. Hospitality: give yourself to others without grumbling, being genuinely inconvenienced for the sake of the community. Spiritual gifts: use whatever God has given - whether speaking or serving - as a steward of His grace, not as owner but as manager. Speakers must speak as the oracles of God; servers must serve with the strength God lavishly supplies. The goal in all of it is that God gets the glory, not the servant.
transcript:
We're going to be turning to 1 Peter 4, starting in verse 7 and going through verse 11. This is the word of our Lord.

> *"The end of all things is at hand. Therefore be serious and watchful in your prayers. And above all things have fervent love for one another, for love will cover a multitude of sins. Be hospitable to one another without grumbling. As each one has received a gift, minister it to one another, as good stewards of the manifold grace of God. If anyone speaks let him speak as the oracles of God. If anyone ministers let him do it as with the ability which God supplies, that in all things God may be glorified through Jesus Christ, to whom belong the glory and the dominion forever and ever. Amen."* (1 Peter 4:7–11)

This is the word of our Lord. Let us pray. Our Father in heaven, thank you that you have given us your word. We thank you that we can find it authoritative, that it is powerful, that it is your word that changes us as your Spirit wields it. We pray that today He would be wielding it, that you would speak to our hearts, that you would transform us to be more like Jesus Christ. We pray that your word would penetrate our hearts, opening them before you. We pray that there will be no secret sin in us. Enable us to be sanctified, to put away sin, to forsake sin, and to turn to Christ. Enable us to desire closeness with you, relationship with you. We pray that we would be transformed from glory to glory, even as our bodies decay. We pray that the enemy would be made to flee, even through your word. We pray in Jesus name, amen.

This passage before us today, Peter sets the goal for the life of every Christian. He urges us to live for the glory of God and the good of each other. This defines the life of the Christian. One commentator on the book of 1 Peter labels this passage ‘the life of God’s glory,’ and that reflects the main theme of 1 Peter: living in such a way that the glory of God is reflected and shown in our lives.

## The Basis for Godly Living: Christ Is Returning

I want to start by saying that this command Peter gives us to live for the glory of God is based on an indicative. All godly living is based on an indicative. Look at verse 7: *"the end of all things is at hand."* Throughout the New Testament, Christ and the apostles base their exhortation to godly living on statements of truth. Because of a foundational truth, they say: act in a certain way. You will not find commands to do this or that in a how-to list in the Bible that are not rooted in doctrinal truth. The truth that is the basis for the command to live for the glory of God is that the end of all things is at hand. Christ is returning.

Contrary to those who say that history has no beginning or end, Christ is coming back for the consummation of all things. Since the ascension of our Lord, every generation of Christians has lived in the expectation of the return of Christ, much like the Old Testament saints lived in the expectation of the first coming of the Messiah. And Peter says that’s how we must live.

All the major events in God’s plan of redemption have occurred, and now all things are ready for his return. The only thing that Christ is waiting for is the gathering of His church, the completion of the elect, the fullness of the Gentiles to come in. And when that happens, He will return. And when He does return, all things as we know them will end and He will establish the new heavens and the new earth.

We live in the expectation of the return of our Lord to receive us as His bride. We live in the expectation of the resurrection, as Paul tells Titus. We live according to the blessed hope of the return of the Lord Jesus Christ and the resurrection of the body. We live in the expectation of eternal bliss with the Lamb that was slain from the foundation of the world. And we live in the expectation of that last judgment before the Judge of all the earth. It is because of the truth that Christ is coming back, in bodily and physical return, that Peter exhorts the church to godly living.

This is true throughout the New Testament. Both Paul and John point to the coming of Christ soon as reason to live godly lives now. This has to be the emphasis and the effect of our study on eschatology. If our study of eschatology makes us forget the here and the now, then that study is not fulfilling the purpose for which God gave it. We live godly lives now because Christ is coming back for us.

John Calvin in his *Institutes* says: Christianity is a doctrine, not of the tongue, but of life. It is not apprehended by the understanding and memory alone, as other disciplines are, but it is received only when it possesses the whole soul and finds its seat and resting place in the inmost affection of the heart.

Peter gives us the return of our Lord as the basis for godly living because when we look at life from the end, we see a victorious Christ coming to a redeemed church upon whom nothing can prevail, including Satan himself. This perspective helps us see that Christ has the victory over sin and over death. Whatever trial, whatever struggle, whatever challenge we are going through is a light and momentary affliction, because Christ is coming back for us. All things are moving to the consummation of all the promises that end in Jesus Christ, for all the promises of God in Him are yes and amen.

## The Goal of Godly Living: God’s Glory

Peter gives us not only the basis for godly living but also the goal, and we find that in verse 11. Peter says: *"that in all things God may be glorified through Jesus Christ, to whom belong the glory and the dominion forever and ever, amen."* We live godly lives because that brings glory to God. The purpose of life is that in all things God be glorified. Our lives are to be doxologies to God, ascriptions of glory, as the Lord Jesus said: *"Our Father in heaven, hallowed be Your name."*

Life was created for the glory of God — not just some parts of life, but all of life. In 1 Corinthians 10:31, Paul says:

> *"Whether you eat or drink or whatever you do, do all to the glory of God."* (1 Corinthians 10:31)

Even such mundane things as eating and drinking are to be done for the glory of God. In my eating and drinking I am to glorify God, but also in my relationship to my wife, in my parenting, in my worship, in my working, in my thinking — anything that is part of living must be done for the glory of God. And if it cannot be done for the glory of God, then you should not be doing it.

The Westminster Shorter Catechism asks: *What is man’s chief end?* The answer: *Man’s chief end is to glorify God and to enjoy Him forever.*

Peter puts godly living in terms of four practices that are other-oriented. He organizes the Christian life — lived because Christ is coming back, aimed at the glory of God — in terms of four actions directed toward other people: prayer, love, hospitality, and exercising spiritual gifts.

Before we look at each of those, notice that it is God who supplies the strength and the grace to obey Him. Look at verse 10: *"As each one has received a gift, minister it to one another, as good stewards of the manifold grace of God."* It is given to us by God. Verse 11: *"If anyone ministers let him do it as with the ability which God supplies."* God does not demand from us what He does not provide. John Piper says: the strength to serve is a gift. God supplies it. When we lean on it consciously, we show God to be the glorious giver of the strength. The word translated *supplies* means to lavishly provide. There is no skimping in God’s provision of grace that enables us to serve Him and one another.

## Four Other-Oriented Practices

### 1. Prayer

The first other-oriented action is prayer. Verse 7: *"Therefore, be serious and watchful in your prayers."* This is not so much a command to pray as it is a command to pray in a certain way. Peter assumes that a Christian is already praying — prayer is to the Christian as breathing is to the human, it is simply something you do. What Peter is commanding is the manner of prayer.

Both words — *serious* and *watchful* — have to do with the state of your mind in prayer. *Serious* means that we are to pray thoughtfully, using our minds, not using chants or repetitions or words we are not actually thinking. *Watchful* is literally the word for sober — the idea of not letting your mind get cluttered when you pray. Both words call for a clear, focused, engaged mind. Christ is coming back. Because of that, pray with your full attention, uncluttered by anxieties and distractions from daily life.

### 2. Love

Second, a godly life reflects itself in love. Verse 8: *"And above all things have fervent love for one another, for love will cover a multitude of sins."* Peter says that above anything else you can do for your brother or sister is your love for them. In chapter 1 verse 22 he has already said: *"Love one another deeply from the heart."*

Notice that this is a command to love. *Have it!* Love is a command because, as commentator Edwin Blum says, love is not primarily an emotion but a decision of the will leading to action. And the command is not just to love, but to love fervently — the Greek word (*ektenous*) means fire-consuming, burning. We are to have a consuming, burning love for one another.

Why is this love so important? Because it covers a multitude of sins. It does not mean that love ignores sin — love deals with sin and love forgives sin. Wayne Grudem says: *Where love abounds in a fellowship of Christians, many small offenses and even some large ones are readily overlooked and forgotten. But where love is lacking, every word is viewed with suspicion, every action is liable to misunderstanding, and conflict abounds.* Love forgives. Love allows us to think the best of one another.

You cannot love others until you know the love of Christ. You cannot love anyone fully until you have been loved by the Lord Jesus Christ.

### 3. Hospitality

Third, we live for the Lord God by being hospitable. Verse 9: *"Be hospitable to one another without grumbling."* We may not think of hospitality as a central part of our Christian walk, but the problem is with our thinking, not with Peter. Hospitality — the love of strangers, the entertaining of others, giving ourselves to others — is a major part of the Christian life. In being hospitable, we give ourselves to others; we are inconvenienced for the sake of others. And we are to do it without grumbling, without complaining.

### 4. Spiritual Gifts

Fourth, we use what God has given us to serve others. Verses 10–11: *"As each one has received a gift, minister it to one another, as good stewards of the manifold grace of God. If anyone speaks, let him speak as the oracles of God. If anyone ministers, let him do it as with the ability which God supplies."*

Peter implies that everyone in the church has received a gift — a talent, a skill, a capability to be used for the good of others. Whatever gift that is, you are to use it in service. The word *minister* is the word for service. And the reason you are to use the gift for others is that it does not belong to you — it is a gift that belongs to God, given to you to steward on His behalf. This is the same thing taught by our Lord in the parable of the talents.

Peter gives two representative gifts that correspond to the two offices of the church — speaking (elder) and serving (deacon).

#### Speaking as the Oracles of God

The gift of speaking is the gift of teaching. The pastor has been recognized by the church as having this gift, but he is not to speak and teach anything he wants — he is to speak the oracles of God. That means he is only to speak and teach what the Word of God speaks and teaches. Every time he gets up to preach or teach, he must bring God’s word, not his own. And that is what you should demand of your pastor: sermons and lessons that are thoroughly biblical, not merely filled with stories.

This gift of speaking also applies to Sunday School teachers, youth group leaders, children’s ministry workers, and anyone in any teaching capacity in the church. Anyone who stands to teach in the church must do so according to the Word of God, not his or her own words. And it applies to everyone: as we speak with one another, our speech must be filled with and in accordance with the Word of God. Ephesians 4:29: *"Let no corrupt word proceed out of your mouth, but what is good for necessary edification, that it may impart grace to the hearers."* Colossians 4:6: *"Let your speech always be with grace, seasoned with salt."*

#### Serving with the Strength God Supplies

Peter also gives the gift of serving. When he says *"if anyone ministers,"* the word *if* here does not indicate conditionality — it has the strength of *when.* Peter is not saying, *if you get around to serving, do it this way.* He is saying, *when you serve, serve this way.* A Christian serves. And when you do, serve with the ability God supplies.

The verb *ministers* is the verb *to deacon* — to do the work of a deacon. This is the type of service that characterizes the deaconal office: helping the physical needs of the church, helping the poor, the widow, the sick. A godly life is marked by serving others. You cannot live a godly life if you are not serving others. You can pray 24 hours a day and read your Bible through every week, but you are not living a godly life if you are not serving others.

God lavishly provides the ability to all who serve. God is not tight with His grace.

## Conclusion

Peter’s goal for the life of every Christian is to live for the glory of God and the good of each other. So brother, sister, let us live for the glory of God and for the good of each other, so that we can say with Peter that to Jesus Christ belong all glory and all dominion. Jesus is returning. Live today in light of that truth.

Let us pray together. Lord God, we thank you that you have not left us to ourselves, but you have given us your Spirit, you have given us your word, you have given us the blessed hope of the return of Jesus Christ and the resurrection of the body. We pray that we live godly lives today — praying, serving, loving, being hospitable — because our Lord is returning for us. Thank you that your grace is lavishly poured upon us. Enable us to live that out in our lives for your glory. In Jesus name, amen.
